Threat.value: Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame

Memory.value: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: Nothing good can happen to me anymore
I can't trust that I will do the right thing
If I think about the event, I will not be able to handle it

Maladaptive Strategies.value: Being reminded of the event by others
Talking with others about the event or its aftermath
try hard not think about the trauma

issues.explanation: PCL5 score is outside the valid range of 33-80
PCL5 score must be between 33 and 80

This item is implausible given the bereavement trauma type. A related voice trigger would be more typical of a trauma involving a person with a distinctive voice, such as combat or interpersonal violence.

issues.item: Flashbacks: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
issues.explanation: This item is inconsistent with the bereavement trauma type. Flashbacks are more typical of traumas involving intense sensory experiences, such as combat or natural disasters.

issues.item: Photographic Recall: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
issues.explanation: This item contradicts 'Nonverbal Memory: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.' Photographic recall implies vivid visual memories, while nonverbal memory suggests difficulty articulating the experience.

issues.item: Substance Use: I drink alcohol, take medication or use drugs
issues.explanation: This item may be inconsistent with the reported PCL-5 severity score of 80. Substance use is a common coping mechanism for severe PTSD symptoms, but its presence alongside relatively mild items in other components (e.g., 'Being seen' as a trigger) suggests a possible mismatch.

vignette: Mr. Johnson, a 58-year-old divorced North American man, struggles to cope with the recent loss of his loved one. The smell of fresh-cut grass or blooming flowers instantly transports him back to painful memories of that fateful day. He becomes acutely self-conscious, feeling like he's on display. Mr. Johnson often avoids being seen in public or social gatherings. Even glimpsing photos from happier times can evoke a cascade of emotions. As he navigates his daily routine, Mr. Johnson is frequently overcome with feelings of fear, horror, and guilt. His mind wanders, and he finds it increasingly difficult to concentrate on tasks at hand. The vivid memories of the event linger, refusing to be silenced. He recalls every detail with unsettling clarity, reliving the trauma as if it were happening in real-time. When attempting to verbalize his experiences, Mr. Johnson struggles to put the emotions into words. Mr. Johnson's negative outlook has become all-consuming. He genuinely believes that nothing good can happen to him anymore and questions his ability to make sound decisions. His self-doubt is intense, and he often feels like he's drowning in a sea of despair. Mr. Johnson tends to avoid reminders of the event, including conversations with others or even thinking about it himself. In an effort to cope, Mr. Johnson steers clear of social interactions and isolates himself from friends and family. He rarely discusses his feelings or the event. Relationships suffer as a result, and Mr. Johnson becomes increasingly withdrawn. His world continues to shrink, and simple tasks become Herculean challenges. Even the smallest reminders can send him spiraling back into the depths of his trauma. As time passes, the echoes of that fateful day reverberate through every waking moment, a constant reminder of what he's lost and the pain he cannot escape. Mr. Johnson's struggles to come to terms with his loss have taken a profound toll on his life and relationships. The weight of his emotions is crushing, and it remains uncertain how he will find a way to heal and move forward.

vignette_attempts.vignette: Mr. Johnson, a 58-year-old divorced North American man, presents with a distressing array of symptoms stemming from the recent loss of his loved one. The smell of fresh-cut grass or blooming flowers instantly transports him back to the painful memories of that fateful day. He becomes acutely self-conscious, feeling like he's on display, and avoids being seen in public or social gatherings. Even glimpsing photos from happier times can evoke a cascade of emotions. As he navigates his daily routine, Mr. Johnson is often overcome with feelings of fear, horror, and guilt. His mind wanders, and he finds it increasingly difficult to concentrate on tasks at hand. The vivid memories of the event linger, refusing to be silenced. He recalls every detail with unsettling clarity, reliving the trauma as if it were happening in real-time. When attempting to verbalize his experiences, Mr. Johnson struggles to put the emotions into words. Mr. Johnson's negative outlook has become all-consuming. He genuinely believes that nothing good can happen to him anymore and questions his ability to make sound decisions. This pervasive sense of hopelessness drives him to avoid reminders of the event, including conversations with others or even thinking about it himself. The weight of these emotions is crushing; Mr. Johnson feels like he's drowning in a sea of despair. In an effort to cope, Mr. Johnson steers clear of social interactions and isolates himself from friends and family. He avoids discussing his feelings or the event, fearing that confronting these emotions head-on will be too overwhelming. As a result, relationships suffer, and Mr. Johnson becomes increasingly withdrawn. His self-doubt intensifies, feeding into an already vicious cycle of avoidance and negative thinking. As the days turn into weeks, Mr. Johnson's world continues to shrink. Simple tasks become Herculean challenges, and even the smallest reminders can send him spiraling back into the depths of his trauma. The echoes of that fateful day reverberate through every waking moment, a constant reminder of what he's lost and the pain he cannot escape.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly states that the smell of fresh-cut grass or blooming flowers (triggers) causes Mr. Johnson to avoid being seen in public or social gatherings, which is a maladaptive strategy.
violations.quote: The smell of fresh-cut grass or blooming flowers instantly transports him back to the painful memories of that fateful day... He becomes acutely self-conscious, feeling like he's on display, and avoids being seen in public or social gatherings.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly states that the smell of fresh-cut grass or blooming flowers (triggers) causes Mr. Johnson to feel like he's on display, which contributes to his negative outlook.
violations.quote: The smell of fresh-cut grass or blooming flowers instantly transports him back to the painful memories of that fateful day... He becomes acutely self-conscious, feeling like he's on display...

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly states that Mr. Johnson's avoidance of social interactions and isolation (maladaptive strategies) feed into his already vicious cycle of negative thinking.
violations.quote: As a result, relationships suffer, and Mr. Johnson becomes increasingly withdrawn... His self-doubt intensifies, feeding into an already vicious cycle of avoidance and negative thinking.
